Snowfall of 5 to 10 inches was accompanied by winds strong enough to reduce visibilities to a quarter mile or less at times in blowing snow. The winds also caused drifting snow, which contributed to making travel very difficult. In addition, dangerous wind chills developed late in the storm as actual temperatures fell. Numerous schools were closed or delayed. Several after school activities, mostly basketball games, were postponed.

Widespread snowfall of 5 to 8 inches was preceded by freezing rain in many areas, leaving an icy layer beneath the snow on roads and making travel extremely difficult. Numerous vehicles slid off roads and into ditches, and there were several accidents. Winds gusting up to 30 mph caused drifting snow which added to the travel difficulties. Classes were cancelled or delayed at several schools.

Snowfall of 3 to 5 inches, along with drifting of the new and previously existing snowpack, caused travel delays. The winter conditions also delayed or cancelled classes and athletic events at some schools.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 44 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

Heavy snow produced accumulations of 6 to 17 inches. The 17 inch report was from Dakota Dunes in the extreme southeast corner of the state. Numerous other reports of 10 inches or more were received over the area along and just north of the Missouri River including Platte...Armour...Avon...Tyndall...Yankton... Vermillion...and other locations. Numerous schools and several businesses were closed for the day. Travel was brought to a standstill for a while in some areas.

A strong cold front moved quickly through western South Dakota early in the day. Across northwest South Dakota, northwest winds increased late in the morning to 35 to 45 mph, with gusts around 60 mph. Winds began to taper off by early evening.

A strong cold front moved through early in the day, bringing much colder air into the area. An upper level disturbance then crossed the area during the night, bringing local heavy snows to the northern Black Hills and the northern foothills. Much of this area received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with amounts of up to 8 inches in Lead, Deadwood, and areas south and west of Spearfish.

A powerful upper level storm system moved slowly across the Central Rockies and then through the Northern and Central Plains, bringing abundant precipitation and heavy snow to much of Western and South Central South Dakota. The storm began to affect the region on the 28th as rain and snow developed across far Western South Dakota late in the afternoon. Precipitation changed over to all snow during the evening hours and then continued through the overnight. Precipitation tapered off in the morning. Then as low pressure began to move south and east of the region, precipitation moved back northward across all of Western and South Central South Dakota on the afternoon of the 29th and continued into the morning hours of March 1st. Rain changed over to snow across Central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 29th. As low pressure moved away from the area, strong northwest winds developed late on the 29th and continued through the afternoon of March 1st. The winds caused significant blowing and drifting of the snow and created near blizzard conditions at times. Travel became nearly impossible across the area and Interstate 90 across Western South Dakota was closed to travel on the morning of March 1st. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were common across the Western South Dakota Plains. 10 to 20 inches fell across the Northern and Eastern Foothills, as well as much of the Black Hills. Snow drifts up to 6 feet high developed across parts of the Plains due to the strong winds.

A strong cold front moved across the Northern Plains, bringing snow and very strong northwest winds behind it. 1 to 3 inches of new snowfall was common across Northwest South Dakota, with 3 to 7 inches across the Northern Black Hills. The snow, combined with northwest winds of 25 to 35 mph and gusts to around 50 mph,  created considerable blowing and drifting of snow across Northwest South Dakota. Visibilities were reduced to less than a quarter of a mile at times. The ground blizzard conditions persisted for about 24 hours.

Heavy snow, beginning on February 29th, continued into March 1st and ended late in the morning. Some snowfall amounts included, 6 inches at Isabel and Ree Heights, 7 inches at Gettysburg and Eagle Butte, 8 inches at Murdo, and 9 inches northeast of Isabel in southern Corson County.

A strong cold front moved through early in the day, bringing much colder air into the area. An upper level disturbance then crossed the area during the night, bringing local heavy snows to the northern Black Hills and the northern foothills. Much of this area received 2 to 4 inches of snow, with amounts of up to 8 inches in Lead, Deadwood, and areas south and west of Spearfish.

A powerful upper level storm system moved slowly across the Central Rockies and then through the Northern and Central Plains, bringing abundant precipitation and heavy snow to much of Western and South Central South Dakota. The storm began to affect the region on the 28th as rain and snow developed across far Western South Dakota late in the afternoon. Precipitation changed over to all snow during the evening hours and then continued through the overnight. Precipitation tapered off in the morning. Then as low pressure began to move south and east of the region, precipitation moved back northward across all of Western and South Central South Dakota on the afternoon of the 29th and continued into the morning hours of March 1st. Rain changed over to snow across Central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 29th. As low pressure moved away from the area, strong northwest winds developed late on the 29th and continued through the afternoon of March 1st. The winds caused significant blowing and drifting of the snow and created near blizzard conditions at times. Travel became nearly impossible across the area and Interstate 90 across Western South Dakota was closed to travel on the morning of March 1st. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were common across the Western South Dakota Plains. 10 to 20 inches fell across the Northern and Eastern Foothills, as well as much of the Black Hills. Snow drifts up to 6 feet high developed across parts of the Plains due to the strong winds.
3 to 6 inches of snow fell, causing travel difficulties. A few vehicles slid into ditches in the Mitchell area.
3 to 6 inches of snow fell, causing travel difficulties. A few vehicles slid into ditches in the Mitchell area.

Heavy snow produced accumulations of 6 to 17 inches. The 17 inch report was from Dakota Dunes in the extreme southeast corner of the state. Numerous other reports of 10 inches or more were received over the area along and just north of the Missouri River including Platte...Armour...Avon...Tyndall...Yankton... Vermillion...and other locations. Numerous schools and several businesses were closed for the day. Travel was brought to a standstill for a while in some areas.

A fast moving cold front raced across western South Dakota during the early morning. After the passage of the front, northwest winds quickly increased to 30 to 45 mph, with gusts around 60 mph, across northwest South Dakota. Buffalo reported a wind gust of 67 mph in the afternoon. The strong winds persisted into the early evening before diminishing.

Strong northwest winds, gusting as high as 44 mph, combined with heavy snow cover to cause widespread blowing and drifting snow. Visibilities were frequently below a half mile in open areas. The drifting and low visibilities made travel hazardous, and slippery roads caused by the drifting added to the hazard. Numerous schools either cancelled classes or released students early. Light snow accumulated to less than a half inch, but added to the visibility reduction at times.

A powerful upper level storm system moved slowly across the Central Rockies and then through the Northern and Central Plains, bringing abundant precipitation and heavy snow to much of Western and South Central South Dakota. The storm began to affect the region on the 28th as rain and snow developed across far Western South Dakota late in the afternoon. Precipitation changed over to all snow during the evening hours and then continued through the overnight. Precipitation tapered off in the morning. Then as low pressure began to move south and east of the region, precipitation moved back northward across all of Western and South Central South Dakota on the afternoon of the 29th and continued into the morning hours of March 1st. Rain changed over to snow across Central South Dakota during the evening hours of the 29th. As low pressure moved away from the area, strong northwest winds developed late on the 29th and continued through the afternoon of March 1st. The winds caused significant blowing and drifting of the snow and created near blizzard conditions at times. Travel became nearly impossible across the area and Interstate 90 across Western South Dakota was closed to travel on the morning of March 1st. Snowfall totals of 6 to 12 inches were common across the Western South Dakota Plains. 10 to 20 inches fell across the Northern and Eastern Foothills, as well as much of the Black Hills. Snow drifts up to 6 feet high developed across parts of the Plains due to the strong winds.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall of 3 to 5 inches caused flooding of basements which resulted in damage to property in the basements, mainly in the southwestern part of Sioux Falls. Some apartments and duplexes were also flooded on the lower levels. Damage included waterlogged carpets, furniture, and clothing. Elsewhere over the northern part of the county, roads and fields were flooded. The field flooding was expected to result in damage to crops, but the amount of crops damage could not be determined.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms moved across northeastern parts of Todd County and central Tripp County, producing hail to the size of golf balls and heavy rains. Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were common across these areas, with Okreek and Witten receiving around 8 inches of rain during the evening. Water in creeks across the area rose to bank full with many roads washed out or under water during the overnight. Lightning also struck the Okreek Community Center, completely destroying the building.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: 3 to 6 inches of rain caused flooding of basements, and resulted in damage to contents, especially in Sioux Falls. Several apartments and duplexes were also flooded on the lower levels. The damage included waterlogged carpets, furniture, clothing, and television sets. At least 17 families were forced to move temporarily, according to the Red Cross, who aided the families. Flooding damaged roads in Sioux Falls, and made some roads impassable for several hours. Elsewhere over the southern part of the county, roads and fields were flooded. Crop damage was suspected from the field flooding, but the amount of damage was not known.

Winds gusting to 80 mph caused some damage in and around Glenham. Part of a cattle shed was blown into some power lines which in turn downed two power poles. Some trees and branches were also downed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed across the Central Black Hills and quickly became severe as they moved slowly to the southeast. Hail to the size of golf balls fell from east of Hill City to Mt. Rushmore, and then southeastward into northern parts of Custer State Park. Strong downdraft winds occurred with the hail and damaged some roofs, siding, decks, windows, and vehicles east of Hill City. No one observed the event to estimate the wind speeds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A supercell thunderstorm developed between Johnson Siding and Rapid City, then moved east-southeast through Rapid City, southeast Pennington County, far eastern Custer County, and Jackson County. Rapid Creek went out of its banks near Creek Drive in Rapid City. No injuries or damage were reported.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding of the Big Sioux River continued from the end of May, with farmland and other fields flooded. After cresting in the area on the second of June, the river dropped below flood stage the next day.

Rainfall of 3 to 8 inches caused flooding of numerous basements. Structural damaged was caused to several of the basements as walls caved in. Ground floors of several residences, businesses, and schools were flooded, including  a dentists office. There was significant damage to contents in the flooded buildings, including carpets, furniture, clothing, electronic equipment, and electric wiring. A mobile home park was especially hard hit, with all 37 units in the park flooded, and some units completely washed out. Many roads were flooded, and several were partly washed out, mainly at intersections. One parking lot caved in, with a driver barely escaping in his vehicle. Water and sewer lines were damaged during the cave-ins of the lot and of the basements. Several vehicles were damaged by flooding. Several parks in the city were flooded and closed, as was the zoo.
Rainfall of 3 to 6 inches flooded several roads, yards, and other low areas. Basement flooding was reported in southern sections of Sioux Falls with damage to carpets, furniture, and other contents.
Heavy rainfall caused widespread street flooding, with some manhole covers blown off. Several vehicles were stalled.
Wind downed flagpole, and damaged shed and pole barn of an ag-business east of Onida.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: This tornado destroyed 3 barns and numerous other buildings at a farm, downed power lines, and broke windows in an adjacent home. There were no injuries reported. Several eyewitness reports were received of this tornado.

Severe thunderstorms moved across northeastern parts of Todd County and central Tripp County, producing hail to the size of golf balls and heavy rains. Rainfall totals of 3 to 5 inches were common across these areas, with Okreek and Witten receiving around 8 inches of rain during the evening. Water in creeks across the area rose to bank full with many roads washed out or under water during the overnight. Lightning also struck the Okreek Community Center, completely destroying the building.

Large hail combined with strong winds caused quite a bit of crop and structure destruction from southwest Corson county across northern Dewey county. Crop losses were extensive in a wide band from north of Isabel to south of Timber Lake. Many vehicles, windows, siding, and roofs were damaged by the large hail. Many trees were stripped of their leaves with countless branches and trees downed. Some power lines and poles were also downed causing power outages. Due to the slippery conditions, some vehicles slid into the ditch. Snowplows were dispatched to clear the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system developed and moved across the Central Plains, bringing heavy snow to parts of the Northern Black Hills. Rain and snow began to fall during the morning hours across Northeast Wyoming and the Black Hills. The heaviest snows fell across the Northern Black Hills, where precipitation became upslope enhanced in the afternoon and early evening. The highest snowfall totals were reported in the Lead and Deadwood area, where 6 to 10 inches fell.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The storm entered South Dakota from Traverse county, Minnesota where it produced a tornado. The storm produced a second tornado as it crossed Lake Traverse into South Dakota. This tornado was stronger and destroyed a house, a mobile home, and a travel trailer. Another house and travel trailer were damaged and a shed was also ripped apart.  The tornado also killed three cattle.

Some storage sheds were blown over, skirting was blown off of many trailer houses, and many trees, branches, and power lines along with some poles were downed. The roofs on at least 50 homes and businesses were damaged in Fort Pierre with one house losing a large part of it's roof. Also in Fort Pierre, two light poles were downed, some business signs were damaged or destroyed, a trailer of a traveling harvest group was tipped over, and 11 of 14 doors on a storage facility were blown out.

A line of severe thunderstorms moved into northwest South Dakota during the overnight hours. The line developed into a bow echo as it passed across the area, producing a widespread area of 60 to 80 mph winds along with some hail. The combination of strong winds and hail resulted in broken windows across parts of the area. The hail and wind also resulted in the death of wildlife and some crop damage.

A supercell thunderstorm developed across central Custer County and moved southeastward across eastern Custer County and Shannon County. The storm produced hail to the size of quarters and wind gusts to 70 mph as it moved across Custer County. As the storm moved across Shannon County, it intensified and dropped baseball sized hail in Manderson and golf ball sized hail in Porcupine, before moving into Nebraska.  Damage to automobiles occurred with the larger hail.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew down power lines, causing a power outage. The winds also caused tree damage, with several trees blown down.
Thunderstorm winds caused widespread tree, power line, and crop damage. At least one large tree was uprooted, and numerous 3 to 4 inch diameter limbs were blown down.
Thunderstorm winds damaged trees and power lines, resulting in power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Large hail combined with strong winds caused quite a bit of crop and structure destruction from southwest Corson county across northern Dewey county. Crop losses were extensive in a wide band from north of Isabel to south of Timber Lake. Many vehicles, windows, siding, and roofs were damaged by the large hail. Many trees were stripped of their leaves with countless branches and trees downed. Some power lines and poles were also downed causing power outages. At a farm southwest of Timber Lake, half the roof was torn off of an 80 foot barn and two tractors were damaged. The hail piled up to 6 to 10 inches on Highway 20 west of Timber Lake. Due to the slippery conditions, some vehicles slid into the ditch. Snowplows were dispatched to clear the roads.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large hail covered the ground.

Thunderstorm winds severely damaged a large metal framed storage building, destroyed a 50 thousand bushel grain elevator, and destroyed the press box at a football field. the winds also severely damaged a large recreational building, blew down road signs, and damaged siding and roofs. Holes were left in several homes and city buildings. A brick wall of the old part of the Garretson School was damaged along its length. The winds caused widespread tree damage. Tree debris blocked most roads in Garretson. Power lines and poles were blown down, resulting in power outages which affected both the town and nearby rural areas. Several vehicles were damaged by flying or falling debris. The winds caused widespread crop damage in the area, but the amount of crop damage could not be determined.
Heavy rain caused flooding of roads and other low areas.

Power lines were blown down, resulting in a power outage. The winds damaged crops, especially corn, but the amount of crop damage could not be determined.

Thunderstorm winds blew out the wall of an antique barn at a garden center after blowing out the hayloft doors of the barn and pulling steel anchors from the concrete foundation. The winds also destroyed two greenhouses at the center, with numerous plants inside the greenhouses destroyed or damaged.
